title: Trigeminal Neuralgia
oneLiner: Brief, severe, unilateral electric-shock facial pain in trigeminal distribution; carbamazepine-responsive; needs MRI to exclude secondary causes.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA)** – can cause permanent blindness within hours. **Single finding:** jaw claudication + headache + tender temporal artery + ESR/CRP markedly elevated.
- **Acute angle closure glaucoma** – rapid pressure rise can irreversibly damage optic nerve. **Single finding:** red eye with cloudy cornea, fixed mid-dilated pupil, pain on palpation, raised intraocular pressure on tonometry.
- **Cerebellopontine angle tumour (e.g., vestibular schwannoma)** – may cause hydrocephalus or brainstem compression. **Single finding:** other cranial nerve signs (hearing loss, vertigo, ataxia, facial numbness) – TN is not "pure" if other signs present.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Pain: sudden, severe, lancinating, electric-shock, stabbing – unilateral
  - Distribution: maxillary (V2) and/or mandibular (V3) – rarely V1 alone
  - Triggered by light touch, eating, talking, brushing teeth, cold wind
  - Paroxysmal: lasts seconds to 2 minutes; can occur in clusters
  - Remissions possible (weeks to months)
  - No autonomic features (no lacrimation, rhinorrhoea – key vs. cluster headache)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Cranial nerve exam normal (including corneal reflex) – sensory loss suggests secondary cause (mass, MS)
  - Identify trigger zones (e.g., nasolabial fold, lip, gum)
  - No motor weakness (trigeminal motor branch usually spared)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  | Diagnosis | Differentiator |
  |-----------|----------------|
  | **Dental pain** | Continuous dull ache, worse with hot/cold/percussion; dental X-ray abnormal |
  | **Temporomandibular joint disorder** | Clicking, locking, pain on chewing, tenderness over joint |
  | **Post-herpetic neuralgia** | Prior vesicular rash in same dermatome, constant burning pain, allodynia |
  | **Cluster headache** | Autonomic features (ipsilateral lacrimation, rhinorrhoea, conjunctival injection), more male, periodicity, longer duration (15-180 min) |
  | **Giant cell arteritis** | Temporal headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, systemic upset, raised ESR/CRP |
  | **Pre-trigeminal neuralgia** | Dull ache in same distribution days-weeks before classic pain – diagnosed retrospectively |
- **Splitter:** **Tactile trigger zones + paroxysmal electric shocks → trigeminal neuralgia** vs. **continuous dull pain + no trigger → dental pain**
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Full cranial nerve exam (including fundoscopy, hearing, corneal reflex, facial sensation)
  - Check for red eye (exclude acute glaucoma – do not dilate if glaucoma suspected)
  - Temporomandibular joint palpation, mouth opening
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, ESR, CRP (to rule out GCA, infection)
  - Consider U&Es, LFTs (baseline before carbamazepine)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI brain with contrast (including CISS/FIESTA sequences) – to identify neurovascular compression, tumour, demyelination
  - Second-line: CT head with contrast if MRI contraindicated (less sensitive for posterior fossa)
- **Special tests:** None routine – consider trigeminal reflex testing if atypical
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - ICHD-3 criteria (International Classification of Headache Disorders 3rd edition)
    - At least three attacks of unilateral facial pain in ≥1 trigeminal branch
    - Pain has ALL of: intense, paroxysmal, lasting <2 min, triggered by innocuous stimuli
    - No neurological deficit
    - Not better accounted for by another diagnosis
- **Never-miss test:** **MRI brain with contrast** in all new presentations – missing a cerebellopontine angle tumour or MS plaque is indefensible.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Confirm diagnosis (rule out red flags – no sensory loss, no red eye, no systemic symptoms)
  - Pain control: simple analgesics rarely work. If severe and unable to take oral, consider IV lidocaine or phenytoin [check local pain team] – but usually not needed acutely
  - If GCA suspected (jaw claudication, headache, raised ESR): start high-dose steroids (prednisolone 40-60mg PO/IV) immediately
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Initiate **carbamazepine** (see Drugs & Doses) – start low, titrate slowly
  - Monitor for side effects: dizziness, drowsiness, ataxia
  - Check U&Es weekly for hyponatraemia (especially elderly)
  - If unable to tolerate carbamazepine: switch to **oxcarbazepine**
  - Refer to neurology if diagnosis uncertain or poor response
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Titrate carbamazepine to effective dose (usually 200-400mg twice daily)
  - Consider second-line: lamotrigine, baclofen, gabapentin, pregabalin [check local guidelines]
  - Surgical referral (neurosurgery) if drug-resistant: microvascular decompression, gamma knife radiosurgery, percutaneous rhizotomy
  - If MRI shows vascular compression: neurovascular decompression is first-line surgical option
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Diet: as tolerated. Avoid triggers (cold, chewing)
  - Fluids: IV if nil by mouth (NBM) for surgery
  - VTE prophylaxis: pharmacological if low bleeding risk, otherwise mechanical stockings
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2, then 4-hourly unless unstable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line: Carbamazepine (Tegretol)**
  - Starting dose: **100mg once or twice daily** (100mg nocte for first 3 days, then 100mg bd)
  - Titrate: increase by **100mg every 2-4 days** in divided doses
  - Usual maintenance: **200-400mg twice daily** (max 1.2g/day)
  - Duration: long-term as needed; reassess at 6-month intervals
  - Formulation: standard release (not MR initially) for flexibility
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Oxcarbazepine (Trileptal):** 150-300mg twice daily, increase cautiously (better tolerability but can cause hyponatraemia)
  - **Lamotrigine:** start 25mg once daily, slow titration (risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome)
  - **Gabapentin:** start 300mg once daily, titrate; second-line, less evidence
  - **Baclofen:** 5mg three times daily, increase; third-line
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Carbamazepine: CI with AV block, porphyria, recent MAOI use
  - Potent CYP3A4 inducer – reduces efficacy of oral contraceptives, warfarin, antiepileptics, DOACs, corticosteroids
  - Monitor for hyponatraemia, bone marrow suppression (FBC, U&Es baseline + monthly)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Carbamazepine: mainly hepatic metabolism – reduce dose in severe liver disease
  - Oxcarbazepine: dose adjustment in moderate-severe CKD (CrCl <30mL/min) – [check local pharmacy]
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting too high or too rapid titration** → dizziness, ataxia, falls, hyponatraemia, Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS). Always start at 100mg bd or less and increase slowly (every 2-4 days).
  - **Missing drug interaction** – e.g., not counselling about oral contraceptive failure.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Severe pain leading to distress, dehydration, inability to eat
  - Medication side effects: dizziness, drowsiness, ataxia (carbamazepine)
  - Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis (rare; risk highest in first 2 months, especially in HLA-B*1502 carriers – test if high-risk ethnicity)
  - Hyponatraemia (carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine) – can cause confusion, seizures
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic pain leading to depression, suicide risk
  - Weight loss, poor nutrition
  - Drug tolerance and loss of efficacy
  - Post-surgical complications (facial numbness, hearing loss, CSF leak)
sections.Prognosis:
- Most patients respond initially to carbamazepine but relapses are common; up to 50% eventually require surgery.
- Microvascular decompression has ~80% long-term pain-free success in appropriately selected patients.
- No increased mortality from pain alone – but risk from underlying cause (tumour, MS).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Always examine for trigger zones** – ask patient where light touch sets off pain; this confirms diagnosis.
2. **Check HLA-B*1502** before starting carbamazepine in at-risk ethnic groups (Han Chinese, Thai, Indian) – reduces SJS risk.
3. **Refer early to neurology** if atypical features, sensory loss, age <40, or poor response to first-line drugs – these may indicate secondary TN.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Do not prescribe simple analgesics** (paracetamol, NSAIDs, opioids) – they rarely work for trigeminal neuralgia and delay effective treatment.
2. **Do not start carbamazepine without baseline FBC, U&Es, LFTs** – and counsel about oral contraceptive failure.
3. **Do not delay brain MRI** – missing a vestibular schwannoma, meningioma, or MS lesion is a critical error.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New neurological symptoms (facial numbness, weakness, double vision, hearing loss)
  - Severe headache, fever, or confusion
  - Skin rash (especially if on carbamazepine – could be SJS – stop drug and seek urgent advice)
  - Worsening pain not controlled by initial medication
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP within 2 weeks for medication review and U&Es/FBC
  - Neurology referral should be made within 4 weeks; sooner if atypical
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / specialty registrar if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or ≥3 from baseline)
  - Any new focal neurology (cranial nerve deficit, ataxia)
  - GCS <15
  - Severe hyponatraemia (Na <125 mmol/L)
  - Suspected Stevens-Johnson syndrome (blistering, mucosal ulcers, red eyes)
  - No response to adequate carbamazepine trial after 2 weeks (consider inpatient neurology review)
  - Imaging reveals structural cause (urgent neurosurgery referral)
- **Call crash team:** if anaphylaxis, seizures, cardiorespiratory arrest (e.g., from hyponatraemia or SJS complications)

rawMarkdown:
# Trigeminal Neuralgia

**One-liner:** Brief, severe, unilateral electric-shock facial pain in trigeminal distribution; carbamazepine-responsive; needs MRI to exclude secondary causes.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Giant cell arteritis (GCA)** – can cause permanent blindness within hours. **Single finding:** jaw claudication + headache + tender temporal artery + ESR/CRP markedly elevated.
- **Acute angle closure glaucoma** – rapid pressure rise can irreversibly damage optic nerve. **Single finding:** red eye with cloudy cornea, fixed mid-dilated pupil, pain on palpation, raised intraocular pressure on tonometry.
- **Cerebellopontine angle tumour (e.g., vestibular schwannoma)** – may cause hydrocephalus or brainstem compression. **Single finding:** other cranial nerve signs (hearing loss, vertigo, ataxia, facial numbness) – TN is not "pure" if other signs present.

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Pain: sudden, severe, lancinating, electric-shock, stabbing – unilateral
  - Distribution: maxillary (V2) and/or mandibular (V3) – rarely V1 alone
  - Triggered by light touch, eating, talking, brushing teeth, cold wind
  - Paroxysmal: lasts seconds to 2 minutes; can occur in clusters
  - Remissions possible (weeks to months)
  - No autonomic features (no lacrimation, rhinorrhoea – key vs. cluster headache)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Cranial nerve exam normal (including corneal reflex) – sensory loss suggests secondary cause (mass, MS)
  - Identify trigger zones (e.g., nasolabial fold, lip, gum)
  - No motor weakness (trigeminal motor branch usually spared)

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Most likely → less likely:**
  | Diagnosis | Differentiator |
  |-----------|----------------|
  | **Dental pain** | Continuous dull ache, worse with hot/cold/percussion; dental X-ray abnormal |
  | **Temporomandibular joint disorder** | Clicking, locking, pain on chewing, tenderness over joint |
  | **Post-herpetic neuralgia** | Prior vesicular rash in same dermatome, constant burning pain, allodynia |
  | **Cluster headache** | Autonomic features (ipsilateral lacrimation, rhinorrhoea, conjunctival injection), more male, periodicity, longer duration (15-180 min) |
  | **Giant cell arteritis** | Temporal headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, systemic upset, raised ESR/CRP |
  | **Pre-trigeminal neuralgia** | Dull ache in same distribution days-weeks before classic pain – diagnosed retrospectively |
- **Splitter:** **Tactile trigger zones + paroxysmal electric shocks → trigeminal neuralgia** vs. **continuous dull pain + no trigger → dental pain**

### Investigations

- **Bedside:**
  - Full cranial nerve exam (including fundoscopy, hearing, corneal reflex, facial sensation)
  - Check for red eye (exclude acute glaucoma – do not dilate if glaucoma suspected)
  - Temporomandibular joint palpation, mouth opening
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, ESR, CRP (to rule out GCA, infection)
  - Consider U&Es, LFTs (baseline before carbamazepine)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI brain with contrast (including CISS/FIESTA sequences) – to identify neurovascular compression, tumour, demyelination
  - Second-line: CT head with contrast if MRI contraindicated (less sensitive for posterior fossa)
- **Special tests:** None routine – consider trigeminal reflex testing if atypical
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - ICHD-3 criteria (International Classification of Headache Disorders 3rd edition)
    - At least three attacks of unilateral facial pain in ≥1 trigeminal branch
    - Pain has ALL of: intense, paroxysmal, lasting <2 min, triggered by innocuous stimuli
    - No neurological deficit
    - Not better accounted for by another diagnosis
- **Never-miss test:** **MRI brain with contrast** in all new presentations – missing a cerebellopontine angle tumour or MS plaque is indefensible.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Confirm diagnosis (rule out red flags – no sensory loss, no red eye, no systemic symptoms)
  - Pain control: simple analgesics rarely work. If severe and unable to take oral, consider IV lidocaine or phenytoin [check local pain team] – but usually not needed acutely
  - If GCA suspected (jaw claudication, headache, raised ESR): start high-dose steroids (prednisolone 40-60mg PO/IV) immediately
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Initiate **carbamazepine** (see Drugs & Doses) – start low, titrate slowly
  - Monitor for side effects: dizziness, drowsiness, ataxia
  - Check U&Es weekly for hyponatraemia (especially elderly)
  - If unable to tolerate carbamazepine: switch to **oxcarbazepine**
  - Refer to neurology if diagnosis uncertain or poor response
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Titrate carbamazepine to effective dose (usually 200-400mg twice daily)
  - Consider second-line: lamotrigine, baclofen, gabapentin, pregabalin [check local guidelines]
  - Surgical referral (neurosurgery) if drug-resistant: microvascular decompression, gamma knife radiosurgery, percutaneous rhizotomy
  - If MRI shows vascular compression: neurovascular decompression is first-line surgical option
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Diet: as tolerated. Avoid triggers (cold, chewing)
  - Fluids: IV if nil by mouth (NBM) for surgery
  - VTE prophylaxis: pharmacological if low bleeding risk, otherwise mechanical stockings
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2, then 4-hourly unless unstable

### Drugs & Doses

- **First-line: Carbamazepine (Tegretol)**
  - Starting dose: **100mg once or twice daily** (100mg nocte for first 3 days, then 100mg bd)
  - Titrate: increase by **100mg every 2-4 days** in divided doses
  - Usual maintenance: **200-400mg twice daily** (max 1.2g/day)
  - Duration: long-term as needed; reassess at 6-month intervals
  - Formulation: standard release (not MR initially) for flexibility
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Oxcarbazepine (Trileptal):** 150-300mg twice daily, increase cautiously (better tolerability but can cause hyponatraemia)
  - **Lamotrigine:** start 25mg once daily, slow titration (risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome)
  - **Gabapentin:** start 300mg once daily, titrate; second-line, less evidence
  - **Baclofen:** 5mg three times daily, increase; third-line
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Carbamazepine: CI with AV block, porphyria, recent MAOI use
  - Potent CYP3A4 inducer – reduces efficacy of oral contraceptives, warfarin, antiepileptics, DOACs, corticosteroids
  - Monitor for hyponatraemia, bone marrow suppression (FBC, U&Es baseline + monthly)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Carbamazepine: mainly hepatic metabolism – reduce dose in severe liver disease
  - Oxcarbazepine: dose adjustment in moderate-severe CKD (CrCl <30mL/min) – [check local pharmacy]
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting too high or too rapid titration** → dizziness, ataxia, falls, hyponatraemia, Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS). Always start at 100mg bd or less and increase slowly (every 2-4 days).
  - **Missing drug interaction** – e.g., not counselling about oral contraceptive failure.

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Severe pain leading to distress, dehydration, inability to eat
  - Medication side effects: dizziness, drowsiness, ataxia (carbamazepine)
  - Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis (rare; risk highest in first 2 months, especially in HLA-B*1502 carriers – test if high-risk ethnicity)
  - Hyponatraemia (carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine) – can cause confusion, seizures
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic pain leading to depression, suicide risk
  - Weight loss, poor nutrition
  - Drug tolerance and loss of efficacy
  - Post-surgical complications (facial numbness, hearing loss, CSF leak)

### Prognosis

- Most patients respond initially to carbamazepine but relapses are common; up to 50% eventually require surgery.
- Microvascular decompression has ~80% long-term pain-free success in appropriately selected patients.
- No increased mortality from pain alone – but risk from underlying cause (tumour, MS).

### Pearls (positive actions)

1. **Always examine for trigger zones** – ask patient where light touch sets off pain; this confirms diagnosis.
2. **Check HLA-B*1502** before starting carbamazepine in at-risk ethnic groups (Han Chinese, Thai, Indian) – reduces SJS risk.
3. **Refer early to neurology** if atypical features, sensory loss, age <40, or poor response to first-line drugs – these may indicate secondary TN.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

1. **Do not prescribe simple analgesics** (paracetamol, NSAIDs, opioids) – they rarely work for trigeminal neuralgia and delay effective treatment.
2. **Do not start carbamazepine without baseline FBC, U&Es, LFTs** – and counsel about oral contraceptive failure.
3. **Do not delay brain MRI** – missing a vestibular schwannoma, meningioma, or MS lesion is a critical error.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:**
  - New neurological symptoms (facial numbness, weakness, double vision, hearing loss)
  - Severe headache, fever, or confusion
  - Skin rash (especially if on carbamazepine – could be SJS – stop drug and seek urgent advice)
  - Worsening pain not controlled by initial medication
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP within 2 weeks for medication review and U&Es/FBC
  - Neurology referral should be made within 4 weeks; sooner if atypical

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior / specialty registrar if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or ≥3 from baseline)
  - Any new focal neurology (cranial nerve deficit, ataxia)
  - GCS <15
  - Severe hyponatraemia (Na <125 mmol/L)
  - Suspected Stevens-Johnson syndrome (blistering, mucosal ulcers, red eyes)
  - No response to adequate carbamazepine trial after 2 weeks (consider inpatient neurology review)
  - Imaging reveals structural cause (urgent neurosurgery referral)
- **Call crash team:** if anaphylaxis, seizures, cardiorespiratory arrest (e.g., from hyponatraemia or SJS complications)
